title: Census

infobox.value: This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=). *(August 2025)*

(from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *[censere](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/censere#Latin "wikt:censere")*, 'to assess') is the procedure of systematically acquiring, recording, and calculating information about the members of a given [population](/wiki/Statistical_population "Statistical population"), which are then usually displayed through statistics. This term is used mostly in connection with [national population and housing censuses](/wiki/Population_and_housing_censuses_by_country "Population and housing censuses by country"); other common censuses include [censuses of agriculture](/wiki/Census_of_agriculture "Census of agriculture"), traditional culture, business, supplies, and traffic censuses. The [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN) defines the essential features of population and housing censuses as "individual enumeration, universality within a defined territory, simultaneity and defined periodicity", and recommends that population censuses be taken at least every ten years. UN recommendations also cover census topics to be collected, official definitions, classifications, and other useful information to coordinate international practices.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [Censer](/wiki/Censer "Censer"), [Censive](/wiki/Census_(feudal_tax) "Census (feudal tax)"), [Censure](/wiki/Censure "Censure"), [Senser](/wiki/Senser "Senser"), or [Sensor](/wiki/Sensor "Sensor").

Compilation of information about a given population

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Volkstelling_1925_Census.jpg/250px-Volkstelling_1925_Census.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volkstelling_1925_Census.jpg)

A census taker visits a family of indigenous [Dutch Travellers](/wiki/Dutch_Travellers "Dutch Travellers") living in a [caravan](/wiki/Mobile_home "Mobile home") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") in 1925.

A **census** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *[censere](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/censere#Latin "wikt:censere")*, 'to assess') is the procedure of systematically acquiring, recording, and calculating information about the members of a given [population](/wiki/Statistical_population "Statistical population"), which are then usually displayed through statistics. This term is used mostly in connection with [national population and housing censuses](/wiki/Population_and_housing_censuses_by_country "Population and housing censuses by country"); other common censuses include [censuses of agriculture](/wiki/Census_of_agriculture "Census of agriculture"), traditional culture, business, supplies, and traffic censuses. The [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN) defines the essential features of population and housing censuses as "individual enumeration, universality within a defined territory, simultaneity and defined periodicity", and recommends that population censuses be taken at least every ten years. UN recommendations also cover census topics to be collected, official definitions, classifications, and other useful information to coordinate international practices.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")'s [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (FAO), in turn, defines the census of agriculture as "a statistical operation for collecting, processing and disseminating data on the structure of agriculture, covering the whole or a significant part of a country." "In a census of agriculture, data are collected at the holding level."[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

The word is of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") origin: during the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), the census was a list of all adult males fit for military service. The modern census is essential to [international comparisons](/wiki/International_comparisons "International comparisons") of any type of statistics, and censuses collect data on many attributes of a population, not just the number of individuals. Censuses typically began as the only method of collecting national [demographic](/wiki/Demography "Demography") data and are now part of a larger system of different surveys. Although population and [citizenship](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship")[[4]](#cite_note-e048-4) estimates remain an important function of a census, including exactly the geographic distribution of the population or the agricultural population, statistics can be produced about combinations of attributes, e.g., education by age and sex in different regions. Current [administrative data](/wiki/Administrative_data "Administrative data") systems allow for other approaches to enumeration with the same level of detail but raise concerns about privacy and the possibility of biasing estimates.[[5]](#cite_note-theforgottenfamily.wordpress.org-5)

A census can be contrasted with [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") in which information is obtained only from a subset of a population; typically, main population estimates are updated by such [intercensal estimates](/wiki/Intercensal_estimate "Intercensal estimate"). Modern census data are commonly used for research, business [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing"), and planning, and as a baseline for designing sample surveys by providing a [sampling frame](/wiki/Sampling_frame "Sampling frame") such as an address register. Census counts are necessary to adjust samples to be representative of a population by weighting them as is common in [opinion polling](/wiki/Opinion_poll "Opinion poll"). Similarly, [stratification](/wiki/Stratified_sampling "Stratified sampling") requires knowledge of the relative sizes of different population strata, which can be derived from census enumerations. In some countries, the census provides the official counts used to apportion the number of elected representatives to regions (sometimes controversially – e.g., *[Utah v. Evans](/wiki/Utah_v._Evans "Utah v. Evans")*). In many cases, a carefully chosen random sample can provide more accurate information than attempts to get a population census.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Lastcensus.svg/330px-Lastcensus.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lastcensus.svg)

A world map showing countries' most recent censuses as of 2020

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Iran

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Iran")]

One of the earliest systematic censuses in world history was conducted during the early [Achaemenid period](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), up until the reign of [Darius The Great](/wiki/Darius_the_Great "Darius the Great") in [Ancient Iran](/wiki/History_of_Iran "History of Iran"). This census, aimed at [financial planning](/wiki/Financial_plan "Financial plan"), [military organization](/wiki/Military_organization "Military organization"), and [tax collection](/wiki/Revenue_service "Revenue service"), spanned regions across three continents: [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). It included data on population numbers, the wealth of cities and provinces ([Satrapies](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap")), precise assessments of agricultural lands, the resources of each region, and other factors critical to determining state finances and planning for governance and military operations.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Egypt")]

Main article: [Census in Egypt](/wiki/Census_in_Egypt "Census in Egypt")

The earliest Egyptian census was the [cattle count](/wiki/Cattle_count "Cattle count"), which counted not people but livestock (especially but not exclusively cows) for taxation purposes. During the early [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt") it was taken every two years; the frequency increased over time. Human censuses in Egypt first appeared in the late [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") and developed in the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt").[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") wrote that [Ahmose I](/wiki/Ahmose_I "Ahmose I"), first monarch of the New Kingdom, required every Egyptian to declare annually to the [nomarch](/wiki/Nomarch "Nomarch"), "whence he gained his living".[[9]](#cite_note-9) Under the [Ptolemies](/wiki/Ptolemies "Ptolemies") and the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt") several censuses were conducted in Egypt by government officials.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Ancient Greece

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ancient Greece")]

There are several accounts of ancient Greek city states carrying out censuses.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Israel")]

Censuses are mentioned several times in the [Biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") narrative. God commands a [per capita tax](/wiki/Tax_per_head "Tax per head") to be paid with the census[[12]](#cite_note-12) for the upkeep of the [Tabernacle](/wiki/Tabernacle "Tabernacle"). The [Book of Numbers](/wiki/Book_of_Numbers "Book of Numbers") is named after the counting of the Israelite population[[13]](#cite_note-13) according to the house of the Fathers after the exodus from Egypt. A second census was taken while the Israelites were camped in the "[plains of Moab](/wiki/Plains_of_Moab "Plains of Moab")".[[14]](#cite_note-14) King [David](/wiki/David "David") performed a census that produced disastrous results.[[15]](#cite_note-15) His son, King [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon"), had all of the foreigners in Israel counted.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: China")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=). *(August 2025)* |

Main article: [Census in China](/wiki/Census_in_China "Census in China")

One of the world's earliest preserved censuses[[17]](#cite_note-Hymes-17) was held in China in AD 2 during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), and is still considered by scholars to be quite accurate.[[18]](#cite_note-Twitchett-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986595–96-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) The population was registered as having 57,671,400 individuals in 12,366,470 households but on this occasion only taxable families had been taken into account, indicating the income and the number of soldiers who could be mobilized.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishijima1986595–96-19) Another census was held in AD 144.

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: India")]

The oldest recorded census in India is thought to have occurred around 330 BC during the reign of Emperor [Chandragupta Maurya](/wiki/Chandragupta_Maurya "Chandragupta Maurya") under the leadership of [Chanakya](/wiki/Chanakya "Chanakya") and [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Rome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Rome")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Meister_der_Kahriye-Cami-Kirche_in_Istanbul_005.jpg/250px-Meister_der_Kahriye-Cami-Kirche_in_Istanbul_005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meister_der_Kahriye-Cami-Kirche_in_Istanbul_005.jpg)

An early 13th-century mosaic in the [Chora Church](/wiki/Chora_Church "Chora Church") depicting the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") and [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") registering for the [Census of Quirinius](/wiki/Census_of_Quirinius "Census of Quirinius")

See also: [Roman censor](/wiki/Roman_censor "Roman censor") and [Indiction](/wiki/Indiction "Indiction")

The English term is taken directly from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *census*, from *[censere](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/censere "wikt:censere")* ("to estimate"). The census played a crucial role in the administration of the Roman government, as it was used to determine the class a citizen belonged to for both military and tax purposes. Beginning in the middle republic, it was usually carried out every five years.[[22]](#cite_note-22) It provided a register of citizens and their property from which their duties and privileges could be listed. It is said to have been instituted by the Roman king [Servius Tullius](/wiki/Servius_Tullius "Servius Tullius") in the 6th century BC,[[23]](#cite_note-23) at which time the number of arms-bearing citizens was supposedly counted at around 80,000.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In AD 6, the Roman legate [Publius Sulpicius Quirinius](/wiki/Publius_Sulpicius_Quirinius "Publius Sulpicius Quirinius") organized a [census](/wiki/Census_of_Quirinius "Census of Quirinius") of Judaea [[25]](#cite_note-25) for tax purposes, which was partially responsible for the development of the [Zealot](/wiki/Zealot "Zealot") movement and several failed rebellions against Rome ultimately ending in the [Jewish Diaspora](/wiki/Jewish_Diaspora "Jewish Diaspora"). The [Gospel of Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") makes reference to Quirinius' census in relation to the [birth of Jesus](/wiki/Birth_of_Jesus "Birth of Jesus");[[26]](#cite_note-26) based on variant readings of this passage, a minority of biblical scholars, including [N. T. Wright](/wiki/N._T._Wright "N. T. Wright"), speculate that this passage refers to a separate registration conducted during the reign of [Herod the Great](/wiki/Herod_the_Great "Herod the Great"), several years before Quirinius' census.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The 15-year [indiction](/wiki/Indiction "Indiction") cycle established by [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") in AD 297 was based on [wikt:quindecennial](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/quindecennial "wikt:quindecennial") censuses and formed the basis for dating in late antiquity and under the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_calendar "Byzantine calendar").

### Rashidun and Umayyad Caliphates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rashidun and Umayyad Caliphates")]

In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the [Caliphate](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate") began conducting regular censuses soon after its formation, beginning with the one ordered by the second [Rashidun](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") [caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph"), [Umar](/wiki/Umar "Umar").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Medieval Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Medieval Europe")]

The [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") was undertaken in AD 1086 by [William I of England](/wiki/William_I_of_England "William I of England") so that he could properly tax the land he had recently conquered. In 1183, a census was taken of the [crusader](/wiki/Crusade "Crusade") [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem"), to ascertain the number of men and amount of money that could possibly be raised against an invasion by [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin"), sultan of [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria").

The first national census of France (*L'État des paroisses et des feux*) was undertaken in 1328, mostly for fiscal purposes. It estimated the French population at 16 to 17 million.

### Inca Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Inca Empire")]

In the 15th century, the [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") had a unique way to record census information. The Incas did not have any written language but recorded information collected during censuses and other numeric information as well as non-numeric data on [quipus](/wiki/Quipus "Quipus"), strings from [llama](/wiki/Llama "Llama") or [alpaca](/wiki/Alpaca "Alpaca") hair or cotton cords with numeric and other values encoded by knots in a [base-10](/wiki/Base-10 "Base-10") positional system.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Spanish Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Spanish Empire")]

On May 25, 1577, [King Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") ordered by royal cédula the preparation of a general description of Spain's holdings in the Indies. Instructions and a questionnaire, issued in 1577 by the Office of the Cronista Mayor, were distributed to local officials in the Viceroyalties of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") and Peru to direct the gathering of information. The questionnaire, composed of fifty items, was designed to elicit basic information about the nature of the land and the life of its peoples. The replies, known as "*relaciones geográficas*", were written between 1579 and 1585 and were returned to the Cronista Mayor in Spain by the Council of the Indies.

## Sampling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sampling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Tehran_Census_1869.png/250px-Tehran_Census_1869.png)](/wiki/File:Tehran_Census_1869.png)

The [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran") census in 1869[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Census "Talk:Census"). Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit) by [introducing citations to additional sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). *Find sources:* ["Census"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Census%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Census%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Census%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Census%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Census%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Census%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2024)* |

A census is often construed as the opposite of a sample as it intends to count everyone in a population, rather than a fraction. However, population censuses do rely on a [sampling frame](/wiki/Sampling_frame "Sampling frame") to count the population. This is the only way to be sure that everyone has been included, as otherwise those not responding would not be followed up on and individuals could be missed. The fundamental premise of a census is that the population is not known, and a new estimate is to be made by the analysis of primary data. The use of a sampling frame is [counterintuitive](/wiki/Counterintuitive "Counterintuitive") as it suggests that the [population size](/wiki/Population_size "Population size") is already known. However, a census is also used to collect attribute data on the individuals in the nation, not only to assess population size. This process of sampling marks the difference between a historical census, which was a house-to-house process or the product of an imperial decree, and the modern [statistical](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") project.

The sampling frame used by a census is almost always an address register. Thus, it is not known if there are any residents or how many people there are in each household. Depending on the mode of enumeration, a form is sent to the householder, an enumerator calls, or administrative records for the dwelling are accessed. As a preliminary to the dispatch of forms, census workers will check for any address problems on the ground. While it may seem straightforward to use the postal service file for this purpose, this can be out of date and some dwellings may contain several independent households. A particular problem is what is termed "[communal establishments](/w/index.php?title=Communal_establishments&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communal establishments (page does not exist)")", a category that includes student residences, religious orders, homes for the elderly, people in prisons, etc. As these are not easily enumerated by a single householder, they are often treated differently and visited by special teams of census workers to ensure they are classified appropriately.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Residence definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Residence definitions")]

Individuals are normally counted within [households](/wiki/Household "Household"), and information is typically collected about the household structure and the housing. For this reason, international documents refer to censuses of population and housing. Normally the census response is made by a household, indicating details of individuals resident there. An important aspect of census enumerations is determining which individuals can be counted and which cannot be counted. Broadly, three definitions can be used: *de facto* residence; *de jure* residence; and permanent residence. This is important in considering individuals who have multiple or temporary addresses. Every person should be identified uniquely as a resident in one place; but the place where they happen to be on census day, their [*de facto* residence](/wiki/Home_range "Home range"), may not be the best place to count them. Where an individual uses services may be more useful, and this is at their usual residence. An individual may be recorded at a "permanent" address, which might be a family home for students or long-term migrants.[[32]](#cite_note-:2-32)

A precise definition of residence is needed, to decide whether visitors to a country should be included in the population count. This is becoming more important as students travel abroad for education for a period of several years. Other groups causing problems with enumeration are newborn babies, refugees, people away on holiday, people moving home around census day, and people without a fixed address.

People with second homes, because they are working in another part of the country or have a holiday cottage, are difficult to fix at a particular address; this sometimes causes double counting or houses being mistakenly identified as vacant. Another problem is where people use a different address at different times e.g. students living at their place of education in term time but returning to a family home during vacations, or children whose parents have separated who effectively have two family homes. Census enumeration has always been based on finding people where they live, as there is no systematic alternative: any list used to find people is likely to be derived from census activities in the first place. Recent [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") guidelines provide recommendations on enumerating such complex households.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the census of [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), data is collected at the agricultural holding unit. An agricultural holding is an economic unit of agricultural production under single management comprising all livestock kept and all land used wholly or partly for agricultural production purposes, without regard to title, legal form, or size. Single management may be exercised by an individual or household, jointly by two or more individuals or households, by a clan or tribe, or by a juridical person such as a corporation, cooperative, or government agency. The holding's land may consist of one or more parcels, located in one or more separate areas or one or more territorial or administrative divisions, providing the parcels share the same production means, such as labor, farm buildings, [machinery](/wiki/Machine "Machine") or draught animals.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Enumeration strategies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Enumeration strategies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Enumerator.jpg/250px-Enumerator.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enumerator.jpg)

An enumerator conducting a census survey using a mobile phone-based questionnaire in the rural [Mutasa District](/wiki/Mutasa_District "Mutasa District") in [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") in 2015

Historical censuses used direct field enumeration and assumed that the information collected was fully accurate, with no measurement error. Modern approaches take into account the problems of overcount and undercount and the coherence of census enumerations with other official sources of data.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] For instance, during the 2020 U.S. Census, the Census Bureau counted people primarily by collecting answers sent by mail, on the internet, over the phone, or using shared information through proxies. These methods accounted for 95.5 percent of all occupied housing units in the United States.[[34]](#cite_note-34) This reflects a realist approach to measurement, acknowledging that under any definition of residence there is a true value of the population[*[gobbledegook](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")*] but this can never be measured with complete accuracy. An important aspect of the census process is to evaluate the quality of the data.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Many countries use a post-enumeration survey to adjust the raw census counts.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This works similarly to [capture-recapture](/wiki/Capture-recapture "Capture-recapture") estimation for animal populations. Among census experts, this method is called dual system enumeration (DSE). A sample of households is visited by interviewers who record the details of the household as of census day. These data are then matched to census records, and the number of people missed can be estimated by considering the number of people who are included in one count but not the other. This allows adjustments to the count for non-response, varying between different [demographic](/wiki/Demographic "Demographic") groups. An explanation using a fishing analogy can be found in "Trout, Catfish and Roach..."[[38]](#cite_note-38) which won an award from the [Royal Statistical Society](/wiki/Royal_Statistical_Society "Royal Statistical Society") for excellence in [official statistics](/wiki/Official_statistics "Official statistics") in 2011.

Triple system enumeration has been proposed as an improvement as it would allow evaluation of the statistical dependence of pairs of sources. However, as the matching process is the most difficult aspect of census estimation this has never been implemented for a national enumeration. It would also be difficult to identify three different sources that were sufficiently different to make the triple system effort worthwhile. The DSE approach has another weakness in that it assumes there is no person counted twice (over count). In *de facto* residence definitions this would not be a problem but in *de jure* definitions individuals risk being recorded on more than one form leading to double counting. A particular problem here is students who often have a term time and family address.

Several countries have used a system known as short form/long form.[[39]](#cite_note-39) This is a [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") strategy that randomly chooses a proportion of people to send a more detailed questionnaire to (the long form). Everyone receives the short-form questions. This means more data are collected, but without imposing a burden on the whole population. This also reduces the burden on the statistical office. Indeed, in the UK until 2001 all residents were required to fill in the whole form but only a 10% sample was coded and analysed in detail.[[40]](#cite_note-40) New technology means that all data are now scanned and processed. During the [2011 Canadian census](/wiki/2011_Canadian_census "2011 Canadian census") there was controversy about the cessation of the mandatory long-form census; the head of [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada"), [Munir Sheikh](/wiki/Munir_Sheikh "Munir Sheikh"), resigned upon the federal government's decision to do so.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The use of alternative enumeration strategies is increasing[[42]](#cite_note-42) but these are not as simple as many people assume and are only used in developed countries.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The Netherlands has been most advanced in adopting a census using [administrative data](/wiki/Administrative_data "Administrative data"). This allows a simulated census to be conducted by linking several different administrative databases at an agreed time. Data can be matched, and an overall enumeration established allowing for discrepancies between different data sources. A validation survey is still conducted in a similar way to the post-enumeration survey employed in a traditional census.

Other countries that have a population register use this as a basis for all the census statistics needed by users. This is most common among [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") but requires many distinct registers to be combined, including population, housing, employment, and education. These registers are then combined and brought up to the standard of a statistical register by comparing the data from different sources and ensuring the quality is sufficient for official statistics to be produced.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

A recent innovation is the French instigation of a rolling census program with different regions enumerated each year so that the whole country is completely enumerated every 5 to 10 years.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In Europe, in connection with the 2010 census round, many countries adopted alternative census methodologies, often based on the combination of data from registers, surveys and other sources.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Technology")]

Censuses have evolved in their use of technology; censuses in 2010 used many types of computing. In Brazil, handheld devices were used by enumerators to locate residences on the ground. In many countries, census returns could be made via the Internet as well as in paper form. DSE is facilitated by computer matching techniques that can be automated, such as [propensity score matching](/wiki/Propensity_score_matching "Propensity score matching"). In the UK, all census formats are scanned and stored electronically before being destroyed, replacing the need for physical archives. The record linking to perform an administrative census would not be possible without large databases being stored on computer systems.

There are sometimes problems in introducing new technology. The US census had been intended to use handheld computers, but cost escalated, and this was abandoned, with the contract being sold to Brazil. The online response has some advantages, but one of the functions of the census is to make sure everyone is counted accurately. A system that allowed people to enter their address without verification would be open to abuse. Therefore, households have to be verified on the ground, typically by an enumerator visit or post out[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]. Paper forms are still necessary for those without access to the internet. It is also possible that the hidden nature[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] of an administrative census means that users are not engaged with the importance of contributing their data to official statistics.

Alternatively, population estimations may be carried out remotely with [geographic information system](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system") (GIS) and [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing") technologies.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Development")]

According to the [United Nations Population Fund](/wiki/United_Nations_Population_Fund "United Nations Population Fund") (UNFPA), "The information generated by a population and housing census – numbers of people, their distribution, their living conditions and other key data – is critical for development."[[48]](#cite_note-unfpa.org-48) This is because this type of data is essential for policymakers so that they know where to invest. Many countries have outdated or inaccurate data about their populations and thus have difficulty in addressing the needs of the population.

The UNFPA said:[[48]](#cite_note-unfpa.org-48)

> "The unique advantage of the census is that it represents the entire statistical universe, down to the smallest geographical units, of a country or region. Planners need this information for all kinds of development work, including: assessing demographic trends; analysing socio-economic conditions;[[49]](#cite_note-49) designing evidence-based poverty-reduction strategies; monitoring and evaluating the effectiveness of policies; and tracking progress toward national and internationally agreed development goals."

In addition to making policymakers aware of population issues, the census is also an important tool for identifying forms of social, demographic or economic exclusions, such as inequalities relating to race, ethics, and religion as well as disadvantaged groups such as those with disabilities and the poor.

An accurate census can empower local communities by providing them with the necessary information to participate in local decision-making and ensuring they are represented.

The importance of the census of agriculture for development is that it gives a snapshot of the structure of the agricultural sector in a country and, when compared with previous censuses, provides an opportunity to identify trends and structural transformations of the sector, and points towards areas for policy intervention. Census data are used as a benchmark for current statistics and their value is increased when they are employed together with other data sources.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Uses of data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Uses of data")]

Early censuses in the 19th and 20th centuries collected paper documents which had to be collated by hand, so the statistical information obtained was quite basic. The government that owned the data could publish statistics on the state of the nation.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The results were used to measure changes in the population and apportion representation. Population estimates could be compared to those of other countries.

By the beginning of the 20th century, censuses were recording households and some indications of their employment. In some countries, census archives are released for public examination after many decades, allowing genealogists to track the ancestry of interested people. Archives provide a substantial historical record which may challenge established views. Information such as job titles and arrangements for the destitute and sick may also shed light on the historical structure of society.

Political considerations influence the census in many countries. In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") in 2010 for example, the government under the leadership of [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") abolished the mandatory long-form census. This abolition was a response to protests from some Canadians who resented the personal questions.[[51]](#cite_note-Ditchburn-51) The long-form census was reinstated by the [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau") government in 2016.

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Research")]

As governments assumed responsibility for schooling and welfare, large government [research](/wiki/Research "Research") departments made extensive use of census data. Population projections could be made, to help plan for provision in local government and regions. Central government could also use census data to allocate funding. Even in the mid 20th century, census data was only directly accessible to large government departments. However, computers meant that tabulations could be used directly by university researchers, large businesses and local government offices. They could use the detail of the data to answer new questions and add to local and specialist knowledge.[[32]](#cite_note-:2-32)

Nowadays, census data are published in a wide variety of formats to be accessible to business, all levels of government, media, students and teachers, charities, and any citizen who is interested; researchers in particular have an interest in the role of Census Field Officers (CFO) and their assistants.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Data can be represented visually or analysed in complex statistical models, to show the difference between certain areas, or to understand the association between different personal characteristics. Census data offer a unique insight into small areas and small demographic groups which sample data would be unable to capture with precision.

In the census of agriculture, users need census data to:

1. support and contribute to evidence-based agricultural planning and policy-making. The census information is essential, for example, to monitor the performance of a policy or programme designed for crop diversification or to address food security issues;
2. provide data to facilitate research, investment and business decisions both in the public and private sector;
3. contribute to monitoring environmental changes and evaluating the impact of agricultural practices on the environment such as tillage practices, crop rotation or sources of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions;
4. provide relevant data on work inputs and main work activities, as well as on the labour force in the agriculture sector;
5. provide an important information base for monitoring some key indicators of the [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") (SDGs), in particular those goals related to food security in agricultural holdings, the role of women in agricultural activities and rural poverty;
6. provide baseline data both at the national and small administrative and geographical levels for formulating, monitoring and evaluating programmes and projects interventions;
7. provide essential information on [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") and for the estimation of the non-observed economy, which plays an important role in the compilation of the national accounts and the economic accounts for agriculture.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Privacy and data stewardship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Privacy and data stewardship")]

Although the census provides useful statistical information about a population, the availability of this information could sometimes lead to abuses, political or otherwise, by the linking of individuals' identities to anonymous census data.[[53]](#cite_note-53) This is particularly important when individuals' census responses are made available in [microdata](/wiki/Microdata_(statistics) "Microdata (statistics)") form, but even aggregate-level data can result in privacy breaches when dealing with small areas and/or rare subpopulations.

For instance, when reporting data from a large city, it might be appropriate to give the average income for black males aged between 50 and 60. However, doing this for a town that only has two black males in this age group would be a breach of privacy because either of those persons, knowing his own income and the reported average, could determine the other man's income.

Typically, census data are processed to obscure such individual information. Some agencies do this by intentionally introducing small statistical errors to prevent the identification of individuals in marginal populations;[[54]](#cite_note-54) others swap variables for similar respondents. Whatever is done to reduce the privacy risk, new improved electronic analysis of data can threaten to reveal sensitive individual information. This is known as [statistical disclosure control](/wiki/Statistical_disclosure_control "Statistical disclosure control").

Another possibility is to present survey results by means of statistical models in the form of a multivariate distribution mixture.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The statistical information in the form of [conditional distributions](/wiki/Conditional_probability_distribution "Conditional probability distribution") ([histograms](/wiki/Histogram "Histogram")) can be derived interactively from the estimated [mixture model](/wiki/Mixture_distribution "Mixture distribution") without any further access to the original database. As the final product does not contain any protected microdata, the model-based interactive software can be distributed without any confidentiality concerns.

Another method is simply to release no data at all, except very large scale data directly to the central government. Differing release strategies of governments have led to an international project ([IPUMS](/wiki/IPUMS "IPUMS")) to co-ordinate access to microdata and corresponding metadata. Such projects such as [SDMX](/wiki/SDMX "SDMX") also promote standardising metadata, so that best use can be made of the minimal data available.

## Boycotts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Boycotts")]

Censuses have sometimes been the subject of threatened or realized boycotts. Political historian Laurence Cooley categorizes census boycotts into those 'motivated by concerns specifically related to the census itself' and those where 'the rationale for boycotting is not to influence the questionnaire design or the enumeration process, but rather in pursuit of aims that are incidental to the census'.[[56]](#cite_note-a362-56) Boycotts of the first kind include Kenya in 2009, when some ethnic groups threatened to boycott the census if they were not allocated their own categories,[[57]](#cite_note-t454-57) and Myanmar in 2014, when nationalists threatened a boycott if the Rohingya were allowed to self-identify.[[56]](#cite_note-a362-56) Examples of boycotts where the census was more of a symbolic target include the 1911 UK census, which suffrage organizations boycotted to protest against women's lack of voting rights, using the slogan 'no vote, no census'.[[58]](#cite_note-x735-58) Other prominent cases of census boycotts include West Germany in 1983 and 1987.[[59]](#cite_note-u962-59)

## World population estimates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: World population estimates")]

Further information: [Estimates of historical world population](/wiki/Estimates_of_historical_world_population "Estimates of historical world population")

The earliest [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population") estimate of the world population was made by [Giovanni Battista Riccioli](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Riccioli "Giovanni Battista Riccioli") in 1661; the next by [Johann Peter Süssmilch](/wiki/Johann_Peter_S%C3%BCssmilch "Johann Peter Süssmilch") in 1741, revised in 1762; the third by [Karl Friedrich Wilhelm Dieterici](/wiki/Karl_Friedrich_Wilhelm_Dieterici "Karl Friedrich Wilhelm Dieterici") in 1859.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/1929_world_population_estimate.png/330px-1929_world_population_estimate.png)](/wiki/File:1929_world_population_estimate.png)

The [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") and [International Statistical Institute](/wiki/International_Statistical_Institute "International Statistical Institute") estimates of the world population in 1929

In 1931, Walter Willcox published a table in his book, *International Migrations: Volume II Interpretations*, that estimated the 1929 world population to be roughly 1.8 billion.

## Impact of COVID-19

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Impact of COVID-19")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/A_Philippine_Statistics_Authority_%28PSA%29_enumerator_interviews_a_resident_of_Molino_Homes_II_Subdivision.jpg/250px-A_Philippine_Statistics_Authority_%28PSA%29_enumerator_interviews_a_resident_of_Molino_Homes_II_Subdivision.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Philippine_Statistics_Authority_(PSA)_enumerator_interviews_a_resident_of_Molino_Homes_II_Subdivision.jpg)

A census taker interviews a resident of [Dasmariñas](/wiki/Dasmari%C3%B1as "Dasmariñas"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), in October 2020.

### Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Impact")]

In the early 2020s, the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") posed substantial challenges: of 155 countries surveyed by the [UNSD](/wiki/United_Nations_Statistics_Division "United Nations Statistics Division"), 77% reported disruptions in their census operations.[[61]](#cite_note-:22-61) The crisis dramatically affected census deadlines. The low supply of [personal protective equipment](/wiki/Personal_protective_equipment "Personal protective equipment") to protect against COVID-19 had immediate implications for conducting censuses in communities at risk of transmission.

The [UNFPA](/wiki/United_Nations_Population_Fund "United Nations Population Fund") requested that even where a census was delayed, census preparations were not cancelled in order to assure that implementation could proceed once the pandemic was under control. While new census methods, including online, register-based, and hybrid approaches were being used across the world, these demanded extensive planning and preconditions that could not be created at short notice.[[62]](#cite_note-:02-62)

Following the UNFPA's recommendation, many countries postponed their census activities.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") postponed the deadlines for census by four months. As of 2025, among the ten most populous countries, [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") are the only two yet to conduct a census.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) The pandemic also affected the planning and implementation of censuses of agriculture across the world. Over 80 countries reported that their census of agriculture activities were either delayed, postponed, or suspended.[[66]](#cite_note-:12-66) The extent of the impact varied according to what stage the censuses were at, ranging from planning to fieldwork and data processing stages. Census delays result in critical data being unavailable for an entire agricultural year.[[66]](#cite_note-:12-66)

Lack of census data also affects policy-making. Researchers can make projections of national trends without updated census data, but estimates are less reliable at regional levels.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The financial implications for statistical operations resulting from the pandemic were more immediately visible in lower income countries, primarily due to the reorientation of funding resources away from census programmes and towards pandemic control activities.[[66]](#cite_note-:12-66)

### Adaptation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Adaptation")]

Countries have tried to adopt administrative registers instead of surveys. In the US, administrative records such as [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax_in_the_United_States "Income tax in the United States") return filings were used by the Census Bureau to enumerate households.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Indonesia and Turkey also leveraged their administrative registers to ease the process.[[61]](#cite_note-:22-61)

Due to COVID-19, the United Nations had advocated for the use of technology for regular data collection, concluding how important those activities are for health and housing policymaking.[[69]](#cite_note-69) More countries are adapting newer AI-derived technologies to streamline operations. The UN has announced that they will tend to support register-based censuses more given their efficiency, and have planned to achieve this for the 2030 census round.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Modern implementation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Modern implementation")]

Main article: [Population and housing censuses by country](/wiki/Population_and_housing_censuses_by_country "Population and housing censuses by country")

In the 2020 round of censuses, covering the period 2015–2024, 204 countries or areas conducted at least one census. To a greater extent than in previous years, censuses were delayed or not completed in areas that hosted a significant percentage of the global population. This was driven by factors including the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), declines in international funding for census activities, and declining trust in data collection and institutions.[[71]](#cite_note-71)
